title: Sum of Array Elements
description: A cricket scoreboard tracks runs scored ball-by-ball as an array `runs`. The producer wants the cumulative team total at the end of the over but the engine only allows recursion (no loops). Given the `runs` array, return the sum of all elements using a pure recursive approach.
hints: Base case: empty ya last index reach kiya — return 0.
Recurrence: sum(arr, i) = arr[i] + sum(arr, i+1).
Tu helper function bana sakta hai jo index pass kare.

title: Factorial
description: Razorpay's analytics needs `n!` (factorial of n) to count the number of orderings of n distinct transactions on a date. Implement factorial recursively. Return the result for the given non-negative integer `n`.
hints: Base case: n === 0 ya n === 1 → return 1.
Recurrence: factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1).
Memoization tab useful jab same n bar bar pucha jaaye.

title: Fibonacci Number
description: A loyalty app rewards a user whose login pattern follows Fibonacci numbers. Given an integer `n`, return the n-th Fibonacci number where `fib(0) = 0`, `fib(1) = 1`, and `fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)` for n >= 2. Show the recursive definition; memoize for efficiency.
hints: Base: n < 2 → return n.
Recurrence: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2).
Plain recursion exponential hai — memo se O(n) hota hai.

title: Print Numbers Forward and Backward
description: An IRCTC dashboard wants to print station numbers from 1 to N (announcement order) and N to 1 (return-trip order) using recursion only — no loops allowed. Given `n`, return an object `{forward: number[], reverse: number[]}` where `forward` is `[1, 2, ..., n]` and `reverse` is `[n, n-1, ..., 1]`.
hints: Forward: print first, then recurse on n+1 — but tu reverse direction se bhi solve kar sakta hai.
Trick: `printForward(n)` = `printForward(n-1)` then push n. Stack unwinding order use kar.
Reverse: push n first, then recurse with n-1.

title: Sum of Digits
description: A KYC service must compute the sum of decimal digits of a non-negative integer `n` recursively. Return the digit sum.
hints: Base: n === 0 → return 0.
Recurrence: sum(n) = (n % 10) + sum(floor(n/10)).
Iterative form bhi simple hai using while loop.

title: Reverse String
description: A delivery app's name-display module needs to reverse a customer's name string using only recursion (no loops, no built-in reverse). Given a string `s`, return its reverse.
hints: Base: empty ya single char string return as-is.
Recurrence: reverse(s) = reverse(s.slice(1)) + s[0].
Linear in length — har call ek char strip kare.

title: Pow(x, n)
description: An e-commerce coupon engine multiplies a discount factor `x` by itself `n` times to apply a compounded discount. To handle large `n` quickly, implement fast exponentiation recursively: compute `x^n` using divide-and-conquer (squaring). `n` may be negative — in which case return `1 / x^|n|`. Return the result as a JavaScript number rounded reasonably (tests use a tolerance via exact integer/half cases).
hints: Recurrence: agar n even → power(x, n/2)^2. Odd → x * power(x, n-1).
Negative n: convert to 1 / power(x, -n) at top level.

title: Tower of Hanoi
description: Classic Tower of Hanoi: `n` disks on rod `A`, move them to rod `C` using `B` as auxiliary, with the rule that no larger disk sits on a smaller one. Given `n`, return an object `{count: number, moves: string[]}` where `count` is the minimum number of moves and `moves` is the move sequence as strings like `"A->C"`.
hints: Base: n === 1 → just move from source to dest.
Recurrence: move n-1 from src→aux, move n-th from src→dest, move n-1 from aux→dest.
Total moves count = 2^n - 1.

title: Recursive Palindrome Check
description: A loyalty bot rewards users whose order ID string reads the same forwards and backwards. Implement a pure recursive palindrome check on a string `s`. Return `true` if `s` is a palindrome, otherwise `false`. Comparison is case-sensitive.
hints: Base: length <= 1 → true.
Recurrence: agar s[0] !== s[n-1] → false; else recurse on s[1..n-2].
Two-pointer recursive form O(n) time and O(n) stack.

title: Decimal to Binary Conversion
description: A bank's encoder converts a non-negative account ID `n` to its binary string representation using recursion. Return the binary string with no leading zeros (except `n === 0` returns `"0"`).
hints: Base: n === 0 → return "0" at TOP-LEVEL ONLY; in recursion 0 should return empty.
Recurrence: binary(n) = binary(floor(n/2)) + (n % 2).
Tu wrapper function bana ke top-level pe handle kar le n=0 ka edge.

title: Greatest Common Divisor
description: A coupon-merge service computes the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two non-negative integers `a` and `b` using the Euclidean algorithm — recursively. Return `gcd(a, b)`. By convention `gcd(0, 0) = 0`.
hints: Base: b === 0 → return a.
Recurrence: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a % b).
a < b ka case automatically pehle swap ho jaata hai (a%b == a).

title: String Permutations
description: A campaign tool generates all permutations of a short coupon-code string. Given a string `s` of distinct characters (length ≤ 7), return all permutations as a sorted lexicographic array. Use pure recursion (insert-at-every-position style).
hints: Base: length 1 → return [s].
Recurrence: take s[0], permute s[1..], insert s[0] at every position of each smaller permutation.
Final result ko sort kar do lexicographically.

title: Generate Parentheses
description: A code-formatter generates all combinations of `n` pairs of well-formed parentheses (valid balanced strings). Given `n`, return all valid strings sorted lexicographically. Use a recursive function that tracks remaining open and close counts — open available, close available, current built string.
hints: State: (open_left, close_left, current_string).
Base: open_left === 0 && close_left === 0 → push current_string.
Branches: agar open_left > 0 → add '('. Agar close_left > open_left → add ')'.

title: Unique Paths
description: A warehouse robot starts at the top-left of an `m x n` grid and must reach the bottom-right, moving only right or down. Count the number of unique paths. Use recursion with memoization.
hints: State: (row, col). Base: pohunch gaya at (m-1, n-1) → 1 path.
Recurrence: paths(r, c) = paths(r+1, c) + paths(r, c+1).
Memoize using 2D map ya array taaki same (r,c) bar bar na compute ho.

title: Reverse a Stack Using Recursion
description: A logging service holds events in a stack and must reverse the stack using ONLY recursion — no extra data structure (no auxiliary array, no second stack). Given the stack as an array `stack` (top = last element), return the stack reversed (top should now be the original bottom). You may use the call stack as your only auxiliary memory.
hints: Two recursive helpers: reverse(stack) aur insertAtBottom(stack, x).
reverse: pop top, recursively reverse rest, fir insertAtBottom me use kar.
insertAtBottom: agar empty → push x. Else pop, recurse, fir wapas push.
